truth.papers.A68.title: Overview of Lightweight Ferrous Materials: Strategies and Promises
truth.papers.A68.paper_scope: This overview presents strategies for development of lightweight steels focusing on bulk ferrous alloys, covering metallurgical principles, mechanical properties, processing challenges and automotive application prospects.

prediction.papers.A68.title: Overview of Lightweight Ferrous Materials: Strategies and Promises
prediction.papers.A68.paper_scope: Overview of strategies for developing lightweight bulk ferrous alloys, focusing on Fe-Al and Fe-Mn-Al-C systems, metallurgical principles, mechanical properties, processing routes, and automotive application prospects.

truth.alloys.alloy_58al35mn035c.alloys_notes: Can form ferrite-based duplex or triplex (α+γ+κ) depending on heat treatment.
truth.alloys.alloy_657al33mn018c.alloys_notes: Ferrite-based duplex steel used in CAE stiffness study, density 7.27 g/cm³, Young's modulus 181 GPa.

prediction.alloys.alloy_58al35mn035c.alloys_notes: Composition used for both ferrite-based duplex and triplex low-density steels depending on processing.
prediction.alloys.alloy_657al33mn018c.alloys_notes: Ferrite-based duplex steel used in CAE study for automotive body-in-white application.

truth.processes.proc_cr_anneal.description: Cold rolling followed by continuous annealing.
truth.processes.proc_cr_anneal.processes_notes: Typical processing for ferritic IF steel: cold rolling to 1 mm, annealing in temperature range 800-850 °C.
truth.processes.proc_bh_strainaged.description: Annealing, pre-straining (2%) and strain aging (170 °C, 20 min) to achieve bake hardening.
truth.processes.proc_sol_aged.processes_notes: Solutionizing followed by ageing at 500-750 °C to precipitate κ-carbides.
truth.processes.proc_duplex_ann_aged.descripti: Intercritical annealing followed by aging to produce ferrite-based duplex structure.
truth.processes.proc_duplex_ann_aged.processes: Annealing in two-phase (α+γ) region and ageing.
truth.processes.proc_triplex_ann_aged.descript: Intercritical annealing followed by aging to produce triplex structure (α+γ+κ).
truth.processes.proc_triplex_ann_aged.processe: Annealing and ageing to precipitate κ-carbides in austenite.
truth.processes.proc_anneal.description: Annealing treatment to achieve austenite-based duplex structure.
truth.processes.proc_anneal.processes_notes: Cold rolling and annealing similar to standard automotive sheet processing.
truth.processes.proc_thermomech.description: Thermomechanical processing (casting, hot rolling, cold rolling, annealing) to produce sheet.
truth.processes.proc_thermomech.processes_note: Processing route similar to Fig. 13. Exact parameters not specified for this alloy in the overview.

prediction.processes.proc_cr_anneal.processes_notes: Typical sheet processing for ferritic IF low-density steel.
prediction.processes.proc_bh_strainaged.processes_n: Paint baking treatment: 2% deformation + 170°C for 20 min.
prediction.processes.proc_duplex_ann_aged.processes: Processing to obtain ferrite-based duplex microstructure.
prediction.processes.proc_triplex_ann_aged.processe: Processing to obtain triplex microstructure (ferrite + austenite + kappa-carbide).
prediction.processes.proc_thermomech.processes_note: Used in CAE virtual simulation study for BIW weight reduction; density 7.27 g cm-3 and Young's modulus 181 GPa reported.

prediction.interfaces.sample_305mn8al12c_sol_aged.p: Kappa-carbides precipitate in austenite during aging of Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys at 500-750°C, forming coherent interfaces with the austenitic matrix.

prediction.performance.sample_657al_duplex_sheet.co: 14.5% weight reduction in BIW while maintaining torsional stiffness

prediction.characterization_methods.idx_0.purpose: Microstructure observation of deformed triplex steels showing SIP effect.
prediction.characterization_methods.idx_0.key_findi: Shear bands on {111} planes in austenite and regular arrangement of nanosized kappa-carbides (20-30 nm) coherent to austenitic matrix in Fe-(26-28)Mn-(11-12)Al-(0.9-1.15)C steel.
prediction.characterization_methods.idx_1.purpose: Phase fraction prediction as a function of temperature.
prediction.characterization_methods.idx_1.key_findi: In Fe-6.57Al-3.3Mn-0.18C alloy, austenite content decreases and ferrite increases with increasing temperature in the intercritical field, contrasting with standard low-carbon steels.

truth.computational_details.sample_657al_duple: Finite element analysis for BIW torsional stiffness

prediction.computational_details.sample_657al_duple: not explicitly validated against experiment in this overview

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Fe-Al ferritic steels with Al > 6.5 wt.% may undergo short-range ordering, increasing brittleness and reducing formability.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: κ-carbide precipitation in austenitic steels requires minimum Al and C contents; intragranular precipitation needs Al > 6.2 wt.% and C > 1 wt.% approximately.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Addition of Al decreases Young's modulus: for Fe-Al steels, modulus drops from ~205 GPa at 0% Al to ~153 GPa at 10% Al.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Density of ferrite is 7.85 g/cm³ and austenite 8.15 g/cm³; Al reduces density by about 1.3% per wt.% Al.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: In duplex Fe-6.57Al-3.3Mn-0.18C steel, JMatPro calculation shows α fraction increases and γ decreases with temperature between 800–1200 °C.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Low-density duplex and triplex steels exhibit superior strength-ductility balance compared to ferritic steels and many conventional automotive grades, as seen in the banana diagram (Fig. 9).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: The synthesis route for bulk low-density steels includes casting, hot rolling at ~900 °C finish rolling temperature, coiling at 400–650 °C, pickling, cold rolling with 66.67% reduction, and continuous annealing at 850–900 °C, sometimes followed by aging at 400–450 °C.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Table I data sources: uniform elongation (UE) values are from literature; normal anisotropy (r-bar) only available for the ferritic IF steel (1.37).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: Figure 5 shows phase fractions of Fe-6.57Al-3.3Mn-0.18C alloy calculated by JMatPro; the α phase fraction increases with temperature in the intercritical range, opposite to conventional steels.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: Figure 8 shows TEM images of SIP effect in triplex Fe-(26-28)Mn-(11-12)Al-(0.9-1.15)C steel, with shear bands on {111} planes and coherent κ-carbides of 20-30 nm.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_10.value: Figure 14 shows predicted shell thicknesses for HSLA and low-density duplex steel in Golf V BIW, achieving same torsional stiffness with 14.5% weight reduction.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Approximately 8 wt.% Al causes about 10% density reduction in steels; Fe-Mn-Al-C systems achieve lower densities than Fe-Al at equivalent Al content (Fig. 2).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Bake hardening response in Fe-Al ferritic steels: pre-BH yield strength ranges 345-418 MPa and post-BH yield strength ranges 425-498 MPa, with delta-YS of about 78-80 MPa after 2% deformation + 170°C for 20 min (Fig. 4).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Grain size comparison: Fe-Al ferritic low-density steels with ultralow C and sufficient stabilizer (6.8 and 8.1 wt.% Al) have larger grain sizes than traditional IF steel, whereas higher C and insufficient stabilizer (9.7 wt.% Al) steel possesses finer grain size (Fig. 3).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Al solvus of kappa-phase increases with Al content (730-830°C for 5-10 wt.% Al) and decreases slightly with Mn content (765-800°C for 23-30 wt.% Mn) in Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys (Fig. 7).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: TEM observation of SIP steel Fe-(26-28)Mn-(11-12)Al-(0.9-1.15)C deformed at room temperature shows shear bands on {111} planes in austenite and kappa-carbides of 20-30 nm size coherent to austenitic matrix (Fig. 8); pure text pipeline had no image pixels.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Strength-elongation ranges from literature (banana diagram, Fig. 9): Fe-Al ferritic ~550 MPa / ~25%; Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic ~650 MPa / ~45%; Fe-Mn-Al-C duplex ~700 MPa / ~50%; Fe-Mn-Al-C PH steels ~800 MPa / ~20%; Fe-Al intermetallics ~950 MPa / ~10%; Fe-Mn-Al-C triplex ~1050 MPa / ~70%.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Discontinuous yielding occurs in ferritic low-density steels with free C in the matrix, contrasting with continuous yielding in interstitial-free alloys (Fig. 10).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: True stress-true strain and strain hardening rate data for MBIP steel (Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.8C): at true strain 0.0, SHR ~2500 MPa and stress ~500 MPa; at 0.2, SHR ~1600 MPa and stress ~800 MPa; at 0.4, SHR ~1800 MPa and stress ~1100 MPa; at 0.6, SHR ~1900 MPa and stress ~1400 MPa; at 0.8, SHR ~1100 MPa and stress ~1600 MPa (Fig. 11).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: Young's modulus decreases with Al addition: ~205 GPa at 0% Al, ~163 GPa (Rana) or ~190 GPa (Frommeyer) at 6% Al, ~158 GPa or ~178 GPa at 8% Al, and ~153 GPa at 10% Al (Fig. 12).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: Typical processing route for low-density steels includes ingot casting, reheating at 1250°C, rough rolling, hot rolling to 3.5 mm at FRT ~900°C, run-out table cooling, coiling, continuous annealing at 850-900°C, and cold or warm rolling to 1 mm; Fe-Al-(C) and Fe-Al-Mn-C variants differ in coiling and aging steps (Fig. 13).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_10.value: Strain hardening exponent (n) and normal anisotropy factor (r-bar) from Table I: Ferritic IF steel n=0.17, r-bar=1.37; Austenitic steel n=0.29; Ferrite-based duplex n=0.27; Austenite-based duplex n=0.23; Triplex n=0.16. Uniform elongation (UE) values: Ferritic IF 18.5%, Austenitic 65%, Ferrite-based duplex 34.7%, Austenite-based duplex 26.7%.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_11.value: Solid-solution strengthening by Al in ferritic alloys is about 40 MPa per wt.% Al. Al content in ferritic steels should be restricted to less than 6.5 wt.% to avoid short-range ordering and brittleness.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_12.value: Delta-TRIP steels retain delta-ferrite from solidification with Al additions of 3-5 wt.%, giving about 5% density reduction and avoiding fully martensitic HAZ after spot welding.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_13.value: Economic and processing challenges: poor castability due to loss of fluidity, severe oxidation during hot deformation, difficulty in cold deformation for high-Al ferritic alloys (warm rolling required for >8 wt.% Al), and complex recrystallization behavior in duplex steels where austenite recrystallization temperature is much higher than ferrite.
